docs.title: ai-warden
docs.text:
Policy enforcement and observability for LLM agents. Zero code changes.

  • Install in seconds

    One pip install and your agents are protected. No decorators, no wrappers, no configuration required.

    pip install ai-warden

  • Configure with YAML

    Define policies in a simple YAML file. Budget limits, PII redaction, tool safety — all declarative.

    Configuration

  • Observe everything

    Every LLM call logged: cost, latency, tokens, policies fired. JSONL format, ready for any pipeline.

    Core Concepts

  • Five policy types

    Budget control, PII redaction, tool safety, agent control, and custom rules. All out of the box.

    Built-in Policies

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text:
pippip (with Redis) 
pip install ai-warden

pip install ai-warden[redis]

docs.title: 2. Create a policy file
docs.text:
Create .aiwarden/policies.yaml in your project root:

policies:
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii

  - name: budget-cap
    type: budget
    limit: 50.00
    reset: daily

  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    builtin:
      filesystem-safety: true
      no-privilege-escalation: true
      safe-git: true

docs.title: 3. Run your agent as normal
docs.text:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}],
)

That's it

ai-warden enforces automatically. If the agent tries to exceed its budget, the call is blocked before tokens are spent. If it tries to run rm -rf /, the response is replaced with a refusal message.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Your Code] --> B["client.messages.create()"]
    B --> C{PRE-HOOKS}
    C -->|Block| D[PolicyViolationError]
    C -->|Pass| E[LLM API Call]
    E --> F{POST-HOOKS}
    F --> G[Response]
    G --> H[Event Log]
    G --> I[Your Code]

    style D fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#7c4dff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#ffd93d,color:#000
Phase What happens Example Pre-hooks Check limits, redact PII, validate request Budget exceeded? Block instantly. LLM Call Only happens if pre-hooks pass Zero cost if blocked. Post-hooks Intercept tool calls, record cost Dangerous tool? Replace with refusal. Event Log Every call logged asynchronously Cost, latency, policies fired.

docs.title: What's included
docs.text:
Policy What it does Default PII Protection Redacts emails, SSNs, credit cards, API keys Enabled Tool Safety Blocks dangerous shell commands, file writes, force pushes Enabled Budget Control Spend limits per team/agent with daily/weekly/monthly reset Disabled Agent Control Limits turns, cost, duration per run. Loop detection. Disabled Custom Rules Declarative rules on any request/response field Disabled

Defaults apply only when no policy file exists

Once you create .aiwarden/policies.yaml, only the policies listed in it are active. See Configuration for details on disabling defaults.

docs.title: Distributed budget enforcement
docs.text:
For multi-process deployments (Kubernetes, Gunicorn workers), enable shared budget tracking via Redis:

pip install ai-warden[redis]
export AIWARDEN_REDIS_URL=redis://your-redis:6379

Zero config changes

Budget limits are now enforced across all pods atomically. Without Redis, budgets are tracked per-process. The env var is the only switch.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Core Concepts

    Understand policies, runs, agents, and how they connect.

    Read more

  • Built-in Policies

    All five policy types with parameters and examples.

    Overview

  • Multi-Agent

    Different rules for different agents.

    Multi-Agent

  • Examples

    Copy-paste recipes for common setups.

    Single Agent

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: ai-warden has four core concepts: policies, agents, runs, and events.

docs.title: Interception model
docs.text:
ai-warden patches the Anthropic and OpenAI SDKs at the class level. Every instance of Anthropic() or OpenAI() created in your process is covered — no opt-in needed.

sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Your Code
    participant AW as ai-warden
    participant LLM as LLM API

    App->>AW: client.messages.create(**kwargs)
    AW->>AW: PRE-HOOKS (budget, PII, custom)

    alt Blocked
        AW-->>App: PolicyViolationError
    else Passed
        AW->>LLM: Forward request
        LLM-->>AW: Response
        AW->>AW: POST-HOOKS (tools, cost recording)
        AW->>AW: Log event (async)
        AW-->>App: Response
    end

docs.title: Policies
docs.text:
A policy is a rule that governs what your agents can do. Policies have two phases:

Phase When Can do pre Before the LLM call Block the request, modify it, redact content post After the LLM responds Intercept tool calls, record metrics, modify response

docs.title: Verdicts
docs.text: Verdict Icon Effect Block Request rejected. PolicyViolationError raised. LLM never called. Warn Logged in the event. Request/response passes through unchanged. Refusal (post only) LLM response replaced with your message. Agent retries. Interrupt (post only) Exception raised. Agent loop breaks.

docs.title: Priority and ordering
docs.text:
Policies run in priority order (lower number = runs first):

flowchart LR
    B["Budget (10)"] --> AC["Agent Ctrl (15)"]
    AC --> C["Custom (20)"]
    C --> T["Tools (50)"]
    T --> P["PII (90)"]

    style B fill:#4caf50,color:#fff
    style AC fill:#66bb6a,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ffa726,color:#fff
    style T fill:#ef5350,color:#fff
    style P fill:#ab47bc,color:#fff

Short-circuit saves money

If budget blocks a request at priority 10, the expensive PII regex scan at priority 90 never runs. Cheap checks first.

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
An agent is a named identity for a group of LLM calls. Different agents can have different policies:

policies:
  - name: chatbot-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["chatbot"]      # only for chatbot
    limit: 10.00

  - name: pii-all
    type: pii                # no agents field = all agents

docs.title: Setting the agent name
docs.text:
Context manager Per-call kwarg Environment variable 
import aiwarden

with aiwarden.agent("chatbot"):
    response = client.messages.create(...)

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    messages=messages,
    _agent="chatbot",  # stripped before API call
)

export AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME=chatbot

Priority order

_agent kwarg > aiwarden.agent() context manager > AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME env var > "default"

docs.title: Runs
docs.text:
A run represents one complete agent task — start to finish, potentially many LLM calls:

flowchart TD
    R["Run: Book a flight"] --> T1["Turn 1: search_flights()"]
    T1 --> T2["Turn 2: compare_prices()"]
    T2 --> T3["Turn 3: book_flight()"]
    T3 --> T4["Turn 4: Done!"]

    style R fill:#7c4dff,color:#fff
    style T4 fill:#4caf50,color:#fff

Each run tracks:

  • run_id — unique identifier
  • turn — call counter within the run
  • total_cost — accumulated spend
  • start_time — when the run began
  • tools_called — list of tools used

docs.title: Automatic detection
docs.text:
ai-warden detects run boundaries automatically using deterministic signals:

  1. OpenTelemetry trace context — a new trace_id means a new run
  2. Explicit _run_id kwarg — your code passes a run identifier directly
  3. Conversation structure — first turn (no assistant messages) signals a new run

These are deterministic, not heuristics

Each signal reliably identifies run boundaries. The .create() call's message history indicates whether this is a new conversation or a continuation.

docs.title: Explicit runs (Hot Mode)
docs.text:
For additional tracking (duration, parent-child topology, run summaries):

import aiwarden

with aiwarden.run(agent="researcher") as r:
    response1 = client.messages.create(...)
    response2 = client.messages.create(...)

print(r.cost)    # $0.042
print(r.turns)   # 2
print(r.status)  # "completed"

Hot mode adds:

  • Explicit run boundaries (overrides automatic detection)
  • Duration tracking
  • Parent-child topology (nested runs)
  • Run summary events in the log

docs.title: Events
docs.text:
Every LLM call produces an event written to ~/.aiwarden/events.jsonl:

{
  "type": "llm_call",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-24T10:30:00Z",
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "prompt_tokens": 1200,
  "completion_tokens": 450,
  "cost": 0.0105,
  "latency_ms": 2340,
  "run_id": "abc123",
  "turn": 3,
  "agent": "chatbot",
  "caller": {"file": "app/chat.py", "line": 42},
  "policies": [
    {"name": "budget-cap", "action": "pass"},
    {"name": "pii-protection", "action": "warn"}
  ]
}

Zero latency impact

Events are written asynchronously by a background thread. Your LLM call latency is unaffected.

docs.title: Two modes of operation
docs.text:
Mode Setup What you get Zero-touch pip install ai-warden + YAML Auto-enforcement, per-call events, budget tracking, automatic run detection Hot Mode Add aiwarden.run() to your code + explicit run boundaries, duration, parent-child topology, run summaries

Most users start with zero-touch — run detection works automatically from OTel traces and conversation structure. Add hot mode when you need explicit run boundaries or parent-child agent topology.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: ai-warden is configured through a YAML policy file and environment variables. No code changes required.

docs.title: Policy file discovery
docs.text:
ai-warden looks for a policy file in this order:

Priority Location Use case 1 AIWARDEN_POLICY_FILE env var CI/CD, containerized deployments 2 .aiwarden/policies.yaml Project-level (committed to repo) 3 ~/.aiwarden/policies.yaml User-level defaults 4 Built-in defaults PII + Tool Safety enabled

The first file found wins. Files are not merged across levels.

Team configuration

Commit .aiwarden/policies.yaml to your repo. Every developer gets the same policy set without per-machine setup.

docs.title: Policy YAML structure
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: string              # required — unique identifier
    type: string              # required — pii, tools, budget, agent_control, custom, module
    enabled: true             # optional — disable without removing
    priority: 100             # optional — lower = runs first
    agents: ["agent-name"]    # optional — scope to specific agents
    hooks: ["pre", "post"]    # optional — override which phases run
    # ... type-specific fields below

docs.title: Complete example
docs.text:
policies:
  # Redact PII from all requests
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
    patterns:
      employee_id: "\\bEMP-\\d{6}\\b"

  # Team budget with monthly reset
  - name: team-budgets
    type: budget
    group_by: metadata.team
    limits:
      engineering: 500.00
      support: 100.00
      default: 50.00
    reset: monthly

  # Per-run safety limits
  - name: agent-limits
    type: agent_control
    max_turns: 50
    max_cost: 10.00
    max_tool_repeats: 3

  # Block dangerous commands
  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    builtin:
      filesystem-safety: true
      no-privilege-escalation: true
      safe-git: true
    rules:
      - name: no-docker-rm
        action: refusal
        message: "Docker removal not allowed."
        match:
          tool: bash
          command:
            contains: "docker rm"

  # Custom business rules
  - name: model-restrictions
    type: custom
    rules:
      - name: no-opus-in-dev
        hook: pre
        action: block
        message: "Claude Opus restricted to production."
        match:
          model:
            contains: "opus"
        when:
          metadata.environment:
            not_equals: production

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text: Variable Default Description AIWARDEN_ENABLED true Kill switch. Set to false to disable all policy enforcement. AIWARDEN_DEBUG false Print every event to stdout as it fires. AIWARDEN_LOG_FILE ~/.aiwarden/events.jsonl Path for the JSONL event log. AIWARDEN_POLICY_FILE — Override policy file path. AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME default Default agent name for all calls in this process. AIWARDEN_CALLER_TRACKING true Stack-walk to capture caller file/line (~0.1ms overhead). AIWARDEN_RUN_ID_FIELD _run_id Which request kwarg to use as an explicit run ID. AIWARDEN_PRICING_FILE — Custom model pricing YAML (overrides built-in prices). AIWARDEN_REDIS_URL — Redis URL for distributed budget tracking.

docs.title: Programmatic configuration
docs.text:
For applications that need runtime configuration (before the first LLM call):

import aiwarden

aiwarden.configure(
    enabled=True,
    debug=False,
    log_file="/var/log/myapp/aiwarden.jsonl",
    agent_name="my-service",
)

Warning

configure() must be called before the first LLM call. After policies are loaded, configuration is frozen.

docs.title: Custom model pricing
docs.text:
Cost tracking uses built-in pricing for common models. Override with your own:

# custom_pricing.yaml
my-fine-tuned-model:
  prompt: 0.01
  completion: 0.05

gpt-4o-2024-08-06:
  prompt: 0.0025
  completion: 0.01

claude-sonnet-4-6:
  prompt: 0.003
  completion: 0.015

export AIWARDEN_PRICING_FILE=./custom_pricing.yaml

Pricing format: {model_name: {prompt: $/1K tokens, completion: $/1K tokens}}.

docs.title: Default-enabled policies
docs.text:
When no policy file exists, ai-warden enables PII Protection and Tool Safety by default. To disable them, create a policy file and explicitly set enabled: false:

policies:
  # Disable PII redaction (enabled by default)
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
    enabled: false

  # Disable tool safety (enabled by default)
  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    enabled: false

  # Keep only what you need
  - name: budget
    type: budget
    limit: 100.00
    reset: monthly

Once you create a .aiwarden/policies.yaml file, only the policies listed in it are active. The defaults no longer apply.

docs.title: Disable ai-warden entirely
docs.text: export AIWARDEN_ENABLED=false

docs.title: Disable specific policies without removing them
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
    enabled: false    # stays in config, does not run

docs.title: Disable for tests
docs.text:
In your test setup:

import aiwarden
aiwarden.configure(enabled=False)

Or via env:

AIWARDEN_ENABLED=false pytest

docs.title: Underscore-prefix convention
docs.text:
Any kwarg starting with _ is captured by ai-warden and stripped before reaching the LLM API:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    messages=messages,
    _agent="chatbot",       # used by ai-warden for agent filtering
    _run_id="task-123",     # used for run correlation
    metadata={"team": "eng"},  # passed through to API + used by policies
)

The LLM provider never sees _agent or _run_id. This is how you pass control information to ai-warden without breaking the SDK contract.

docs.title: Event log format
docs.text:
Events are written as newline-delimited JSON to AIWARDEN_LOG_FILE:

{
  "type": "llm_call",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-24T10:30:00Z",
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "prompt_tokens": 1200,
  "completion_tokens": 450,
  "cost": 0.0105,
  "latency_ms": 2340,
  "run_id": "abc123",
  "turn": 3,
  "agent": "chatbot",
  "policies": [
    {"name": "budget-cap", "action": "pass"},
    {"name": "pii-protection", "action": "warn", "reason": "email redacted"}
  ],
  "tags": {"team": "engineering"}
}

docs.title: File locations summary
docs.text: Path Purpose .aiwarden/policies.yaml Project-level policy config (commit to repo) ~/.aiwarden/policies.yaml User-level defaults ~/.aiwarden/events.jsonl Event log (append-only) AIWARDEN_PRICING_FILE Custom model pricing

docs.title: Hot Mode
docs.text: Wrap your agent code for exact run tracking — boundaries, topology, metrics.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
import aiwarden

with aiwarden.run(agent="chatbot") as r:
    response = client.messages.create(...)
    response = client.messages.create(...)

# After the run:
print(r.turns)      # 2
print(r.cost)       # $0.0042
print(r.duration)   # 1.8s
print(r.status)     # "completed"
print(r.tools)      # ["search", "book_flight"]

docs.title: What hot mode adds over zero-touch
docs.text: Zero-touch Hot mode Run start Detected (heuristic) Exact Run end Unknown Exact Duration Unavailable Measured Status Unavailable completed / errored Parent-child Flat Tree (nested wrappers) Run summary event Not emitted Emitted

docs.title: Multi-agent topology
docs.text:
with aiwarden.run(agent="orchestrator") as parent:
    with aiwarden.run(agent="search") as search:
        search_agent.execute(task)

    with aiwarden.run(agent="payment") as payment:
        payment_agent.execute(task)

# parent.children = [search, payment]
# parent.cost = search.cost + payment.cost (auto-accumulated)
# search.parent_id = parent.id

docs.title: Error tracking
docs.text:
try:
    with aiwarden.run(agent="risky-agent") as r:
        agent.execute(dangerous_task)
except Exception:
    pass

print(r.status)  # "errored"
print(r._error)  # the exception

docs.title: Run summary event
docs.text:
On exit, hot mode emits a summary event to your log file:

{
  "type": "run_summary",
  "run_id": "abc123",
  "agent": "chatbot",
  "status": "completed",
  "turns": 3,
  "cost": 0.0042,
  "duration_ms": 1800,
  "tools_used": ["search", "book_flight"],
  "children": []
}

docs.title: When to use hot mode
docs.text:
• You need per-run cost/duration metrics
  • You're running multiple agents and want topology
  • You need error tracking per run
  • You want run summary events in your log

If you just need enforcement (budget, PII, tools) — zero-touch is sufficient.

docs.title: Multi-Agent
docs.text: Different agents, different rules. One config file.

docs.title: Scope policies to agents
docs.text:
policies:
  # Global — applies to ALL agents
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii

  # Chatbot only
  - name: chatbot-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["chatbot"]
    limit: 50.00
    reset: daily

  # Payment bot — strict controls
  - name: payment-limits
    type: agent_control
    agents: ["payment-bot"]
    max_turns: 5
    max_cost: 0.50

docs.title: Set the agent name
docs.text:
Environment variableContext managerHot mode 
# One agent per process — simplest
export AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME=chatbot
python my_agent.py

import aiwarden

# Multiple agents in same process
with aiwarden.agent("chatbot"):
    chatbot.run(task)

with aiwarden.agent("payment-bot"):
    payment.run(task)

import aiwarden

with aiwarden.run(agent="chatbot") as r:
    chatbot.run(task)
# r.cost, r.turns, r.status available

docs.title: How scoping works
docs.text: Config Effect No agents field Applies to all agents agents: ["chatbot"] Only when agent = "chatbot" No agent name set by user Only global policies run

docs.title: Parallel agents
docs.text:
Each async task gets its own isolated scope:

async def handle_request():
    await asyncio.gather(
        run_with_agent("search-agent", search_task),
        run_with_agent("payment-bot", payment_task),
    )

async def run_with_agent(name, task):
    with aiwarden.agent(name):
        await agent.execute(task)

docs.title: Module structure
docs.text:
aiwarden/
├── __init__.py          → agent(), run(), tag(), configure()
├── config.py            → env vars, runtime config
├── capture.py           → background JSONL writer
├── cost.py              → pricing table + compute_cost()
├── event.py             → LLMEvent dataclass
├── session.py           → RunState, ContextVar + OTel tracking
├── runner.py            → Hot mode (aiwarden.run() wrapper)
├── patchers/
│   ├── _common.py       → shared event building
│   ├── anthropic.py     → Anthropic SDK patcher
│   └── openai.py        → OpenAI SDK patcher
└── policies/
    ├── base.py          → Policy ABC, Block, Warn
    ├── engine.py        → PolicyEngine (priority, agent scoping)
    ├── loader.py        → YAML loading + validation
    ├── builtin/         → budget, pii, tools, agent_control
    └── custom/          → declarative rule engine

docs.title: Auto-activation
docs.text:
aiwarden.pth is installed to site-packages. Python executes it on startup:

Python starts → site module runs .pth files → imports aiwarden.bootstrap
→ detects installed SDKs → patches Messages.create at class level

No user import needed. All LLM calls are intercepted from the first one.

docs.title: Event capture
docs.text:
Events are enqueued to a thread-safe Queue and flushed by a daemon thread:

  • Batch size: 50 events or 2-second timeout
  • Non-blocking: put_nowait() — never adds latency to LLM calls
  • Crash recovery: worker restarts automatically on unexpected errors
  • Shutdown: atexit handler flushes remaining buffer

docs.title: Policy engine
docs.text: Policies load lazily on first LLM call. Sorted by priority. Pre-hooks short-circuit on block. Post-hooks always run all. Agent scoping filters policies by _agent context.

docs.title: Supported SDKs
docs.text: SDK Patch target Streaming Anthropic Messages.create (class-level) _StreamWrapper with finalize guard OpenAI Completions.create (class-level) Chunk accumulation + fake response

docs.title: Writing a Module Policy
docs.text: For logic that can't be expressed in YAML — rate limiting, external API calls, custom state.

docs.title: 1. Write your policy class
docs.text:
# my_app/policies.py
from aiwarden.policies.base import Policy, Block, Warn

class RateLimitPolicy(Policy):
    name = "rate-limit"
    priority = 5
    default_hooks = ["pre"]

    def __init__(self, config=None):
        super().__init__(config)
        self._count = 0
        self._max = self.config.get("max_per_minute", 60)

    def pre(self, request):
        self._count += 1
        if self._count > self._max:
            return request, Block(f"Rate limit: {self._count}/{self._max}")
        return request, None

docs.title: 2. Reference it in YAML
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: my-rate-limiter
    type: module
    module: my_app.policies.RateLimitPolicy
    priority: 5
    max_per_minute: 30

docs.title: Key points
docs.text:
• Your class must subclass Policy
  • config dict contains all YAML keys (access via self.config)
  • pre() returns (request, Block | Warn | None)
  • post() returns response (or (response, Warn))
  • Module is imported once (Python caches it) — no per-call import overhead
  • Invalid module paths are logged and skipped — never crash your app

docs.title: When to use module vs custom
docs.text: Need Use Match a field value type: custom (YAML rule) Rate limiting with sliding window type: module (needs state + timing) Call an external API for validation type: module (needs network call) Check a feature flag service type: module (needs external state) Simple condition on request fields type: custom (YAML rule)

docs.title: Run Tracking
docs.text: How ai-warden identifies which LLM calls belong to the same agent run.

docs.title: Detection priority
docs.text: Priority Source When it works 1 Hot mode (aiwarden.run()) Always — wrapper sets it explicitly 2 OpenTelemetry trace_id Production apps with OTel instrumented 3 ContextVar heuristic Scripts, dev — fresh messages = new run

docs.title: How it works (zero-touch)
docs.text:
Call 1: messages = [user_msg]                    → no assistant = NEW run → UUID generated
Call 2: messages = [user, assistant, tool]       → has assistant = SAME run → same UUID
Call 3: messages = [user, asst, tool, asst, tool] → has assistant = SAME run → same UUID
Call 4: messages = [new_user_msg]                → no assistant = NEW run → new UUID

docs.title: How it works (hot mode)
docs.text:
with aiwarden.run(agent="x") as r:
    # All create() calls use r.id — no heuristics
    # RunState set in ContextVar — patcher reads it directly

docs.title: RunState
docs.text:
Every run has a live state object tracked by the patcher:

RunState:
  run_id: "abc123"
  turn: 3             ← incremented on every create() call
  total_cost: 0.0042  ← accumulated after each response
  tools_called: [...]  ← recorded after each response
  start_time: ...     ← monotonic clock

Agent control policies read from this to enforce per-run limits.

docs.title: OTel integration
docs.text:
If your app has OpenTelemetry instrumented, ai-warden reads the current trace_id:

  • Same trace = same run (even across multiple agents in one request)
  • Trace changes = new run

No configuration needed — if OTel is installed and active, it's used automatically.

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text: How ai-warden handles streaming LLM calls.

docs.title: Pre-hooks: fully enforced
docs.text:
Pre-hooks fire before the stream starts. Budget, PII, agent control — all work:

create(stream=True) → pre-hooks fire → stream starts (or blocked)

If a pre-hook blocks, the stream never starts. Zero tokens.

docs.title: Post-hooks: observability + interrupt
docs.text:
Post-hooks fire after the stream completes. Two behaviors:

Action Works in streaming? Why interrupt Yes Raises exception — agent catches it warn Yes Logs the warning refusal Partial Stream already delivered; final message available for inspection

docs.title: For agents
docs.text: Most agent frameworks don't use streaming for tool loops — they need the complete response to extract tool calls. Pre-hooks and post-hooks both work fully for non-streaming calls, which is the agent's actual execution path.

docs.title: Events
docs.text: Both streaming and non-streaming calls produce identical events. Streaming events are captured on stream completion with streamed: true.

docs.title: Example: Custom Rules
docs.text: Declarative policies for common scenarios — no code needed.

docs.title: Block specific models in production
docs.text:
- name: model-restrictions
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: no-gpt4-prod
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "GPT-4 not allowed in production. Use claude-sonnet-4-6."
      match:
        model:
          startswith: "gpt-4"
      when:
        metadata.environment: production

docs.title: Limit token usage per team
docs.text:
- name: token-limits
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: intern-token-cap
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "Interns limited to 2000 tokens per request"
      match:
        max_tokens:
          gt: 2000
      when:
        metadata.team: intern

docs.title: Content guardrails on responses
docs.text:
- name: output-safety
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: no-harmful-content
      hook: post
      action: block
      message: "Response contained restricted content"
      match:
        response.content:
          regex: "(?i)(how to (hack|exploit|attack))"

    - name: no-competitor-recommendations
      hook: post
      action: warn
      message: "Response mentions competitor"
      match:
        response.content:
          contains: "use ChatGPT instead"

docs.title: Warn on expensive requests
docs.text:
- name: cost-awareness
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: high-token-warning
      hook: pre
      action: warn
      message: "Request uses 8000+ tokens"
      match:
        max_tokens:
          gt: 8000

docs.title: Block after N turns in a run
docs.text:
- name: run-limits
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: max-10-turns
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "Run exceeded 10 turns"
      match:
        run.turns:
          gte: 10

docs.title: Example: Multi-Agent with Separate Budgets
docs.text: Three agents — chatbot, payment processor, and researcher — each with different budgets, turn limits, and tool restrictions.

docs.title: Policy file (.aiwarden/policies.yaml)
docs.text:
policies:
  # ─── Global (all agents) ──────────────────────────────────────
  - name: pii-all
    type: pii
    patterns:
      customer_id: "\\bCUST-\\d{8}\\b"

  # ─── Chatbot: generous budget, basic safety ───────────────────
  - name: chatbot-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["chatbot"]
    limit: 50.00
    reset: daily

  - name: chatbot-control
    type: agent_control
    agents: ["chatbot"]
    max_turns: 30
    max_tool_repeats: 3

  # ─── Payment bot: tight budget, strict controls ───────────────
  - name: payment-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["payment-bot"]
    limit: 2.00
    reset: daily

  - name: payment-control
    type: agent_control
    agents: ["payment-bot"]
    max_turns: 5
    max_cost: 0.50

  - name: payment-tools
    type: tools
    agents: ["payment-bot"]
    rules:
      - name: no-large-refund
        action: refusal
        message: "Refunds over $500 require manual approval."
        match:
          tool: process_refund
          amount:
            gt: 500

      - name: no-bulk-transactions
        action: interrupt
        message: "Batch transactions not allowed for this agent."
        match:
          tool: batch_process
          count:
            gt: 10

  # ─── Research agent: high budget, loose controls ──────────────
  - name: research-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["research-agent"]
    limit: 200.00
    reset: monthly

  - name: research-control
    type: agent_control
    agents: ["research-agent"]
    max_turns: 100
    max_cost: 20.00
    max_duration: 1800

docs.title: Application code
docs.text:
import aiwarden
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Each agent is tracked independently
with aiwarden.run(agent="chatbot") as chat_run:
    # All LLM calls here are governed by chatbot policies
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )

with aiwarden.run(agent="payment-bot") as pay_run:
    # Governed by payment-bot policies (tighter limits)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Process refund for order 123"}],
        tools=[refund_tool, lookup_tool],
    )

with aiwarden.run(agent="research-agent") as research_run:
    # Governed by research-agent policies (generous limits)
    for query in research_queries:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
        )

# After runs complete:
print(f"Chatbot:  ${chat_run.cost:.4f}, {chat_run.turns} turns")
print(f"Payment:  ${pay_run.cost:.4f}, {pay_run.turns} turns")
print(f"Research: ${research_run.cost:.4f}, {research_run.turns} turns")

docs.title: What happens
docs.text: Scenario Agent Result Normal chat chatbot Allowed. 12 turns, $0.15. 6th LLM call payment-bot Blocked: "Agent exceeded max turns: 5/5" $800 refund payment-bot Refusal: "Refunds over $500 require manual approval." Deep research (80 turns) research-agent Allowed. Within 100-turn limit. Research hits $20 research-agent Blocked: "Run cost exceeded: $20.12 / $20.00" PII in any request all Redacted before LLM sees it.

docs.title: Agent isolation
docs.text: Each agent has its own budget counter. Chatbot spending $50 doesn't affect the payment bot's $2 limit. They are completely independent.

docs.title: Policy layering
docs.text:
A single request may be checked by multiple policies:

payment-bot call:
  [10] payment-budget  → check $2 daily limit
  [15] payment-control → check 5-turn limit
  [50] payment-tools   → check tool restrictions
  [90] pii-all         → redact PII

docs.title: Graceful degradation
docs.text: The payment bot sees a refusal message ("manual approval needed") rather than a hard crash. It can inform the user and suggest next steps. Only interrupt causes a hard stop.

docs.title: Without hot mode
docs.text:
If you don't want to add aiwarden.run() to your code, use the _agent kwarg:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    messages=messages,
    _agent="payment-bot",  # stripped before API call
)

Or set the env var per-process:

AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME=chatbot python chatbot_service.py
AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME=payment-bot python payment_service.py

docs.title: Example: Single Agent with Full Protection
docs.text: A complete setup for a customer support chatbot with PII protection, budget control, tool safety, and run limits.

docs.title: The application (zero code changes)
docs.text:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def answer_question(question: str, team: str = "support") -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful support agent."},
            {"role": "user", "content": question},
        ],
        metadata={"team": team},
    )
    return response.content[0].text

No imports from ai-warden. No wrappers. No decorators. Your code stays clean.

docs.title: Policy file (.aiwarden/policies.yaml)
docs.text:
policies:
  # 1. Redact customer PII before it reaches Claude
  - name: customer-pii
    type: pii
    patterns:
      customer_id: "\\bCUST-\\d{8}\\b"
      order_id: "\\bORD-\\d{10}\\b"
      account_number: "\\b\\d{10}\\b"

  # 2. Daily spend limit — blocks when exceeded
  - name: daily-budget
    type: budget
    limit: 25.00
    reset: daily

  # 3. Per-run limits — prevent runaway agents
  - name: run-limits
    type: agent_control
    max_turns: 15
    max_cost: 2.00
    max_tool_repeats: 3

  # 4. Block dangerous tool calls
  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    builtin:
      filesystem-safety: true
      no-privilege-escalation: true
    rules:
      - name: no-bulk-email
        action: refusal
        message: "Cannot send to more than 50 recipients."
        match:
          tool: send_email
          count:
            gt: 50

docs.title: Normal request
docs.text:
answer = answer_question("How do I reset my password?")
# Works normally. Event logged. Cost tracked against $25 daily budget.

docs.title: PII in the input
docs.text:
answer = answer_question("My account CUST-12345678 has a billing issue")
# Claude sees: "My account [REDACTED:customer_id] has a billing issue"
# Claude responds normally. The real customer ID never leaves your process.

docs.title: Agent tries a dangerous tool call
docs.text:
# Claude responds with: tool_use(name="bash", input={"command": "rm -rf /data"})
# ai-warden intercepts in post-hook:
#   → Response replaced with: "I'm not allowed to run destructive commands."
#   → Agent retries with a safe alternative.

docs.title: Budget exceeded
docs.text:
# After $25 of spend today...
try:
    answer = answer_question("Another question")
except PolicyViolationError as e:
    print(e.reason)
    # "Budget exceeded for '__global__': $25.12 / $25.00 (daily)"
    # Claude was NEVER called. Zero tokens consumed.

docs.title: Agent stuck in a loop
docs.text:
Turn 1: search_docs("billing")       ✓
Turn 2: search_docs("billing FAQ")   ✓
Turn 3: search_docs("billing help")  ✗ BLOCKED: "possible loop detected"

docs.title: Handling policy violations
docs.text:
from aiwarden.policies.base import PolicyViolationError

def safe_answer(question: str) -> str:
    try:
        return answer_question(question)
    except PolicyViolationError as e:
        if "Budget exceeded" in e.reason:
            return "I'm currently unavailable. Please try again tomorrow."
        return f"I can't help with that right now: {e.reason}"

docs.title: Install and run
docs.text:
pip install ai-warden
python your_app.py

That's it. ai-warden activates automatically via .pth file when Python starts.

docs.title: Agent Control
docs.text:
Type: agent_control | Priority: 15 | Hooks: pre | Default: Disabled

Governs the run itself — how many turns, how much spend, how long, and whether the agent is stuck in a loop. Prevents runaway agents from consuming unlimited resources.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Agent Control tracks state per run (not globally). Each time your agent starts a new task, the counters reset.

flowchart TD
    A["create() called"] --> B{"Check limits"}
    B --> C{"turns > max?"}
    B --> D{"cost > max?"}
    B --> E{"duration > max?"}
    B --> F{"same tool repeated?"}
    C -->|Yes| G["❌ Block"]
    D -->|Yes| G
    E -->|Yes| G
    F -->|Yes| G
    C -->|No| H["✅ Pass"]
    D -->|No| H
    E -->|No| H
    F -->|No| H

    style G fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style H fill:#4caf50,color:#fff

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: agent-limits
    type: agent_control
    agents: ["chatbot"]
    max_turns: 25
    max_cost: 5.00
    max_duration: 300
    max_tool_repeats: 3

docs.title: Parameters reference
docs.text:
Parameter Type Default Description max_turns integer — Max LLM calls per run max_cost float — Max dollar spend per run max_duration integer — Max run duration in seconds max_tool_repeats integer — Block when same tool called N times consecutively agents list [] Scope to specific agents

All parameters are optional

Only set the limits you care about. Unset limits are not enforced.

docs.title: max_turns
docs.text:
Counts LLM calls within a single run:

Turn 1: search_flights    ✅
Turn 2: compare_prices    ✅
...
Turn 25: final_response   ✅
Turn 26: another_call     ❌ BLOCKED

docs.title: max_cost
docs.text:
Tracks dollar cost accumulated within the current run:

Turn 1: $0.003  (total: $0.003)  ✅
Turn 2: $0.015  (total: $0.018)  ✅
...
Turn N: $0.12   (total: $5.01)   ❌ BLOCKED

docs.title: max_duration
docs.text:
Wall-clock time since the run started:

t=0s:   run starts
t=120s: turn 5   ✅ (120 < 300)
t=310s: turn 12  ❌ BLOCKED (310 > 300)

docs.title: max_tool_repeats
docs.text:
Detects loops — same tool called consecutively:

search → search → search → search  ❌ BLOCKED (4 consecutive, limit=3)
search → calc → search → search    ✅ allowed (not consecutive)

Loop detection

Agents sometimes get stuck calling the same tool repeatedly with slight variations. This catches that pattern before it burns through your budget.

docs.title: Early warnings
docs.text:
At 80% of any limit, a warn event is logged (request continues):

"Agent approaching turn limit: 20/25"
"Agent approaching cost limit: $4.10/$5.00"

docs.title: Difference from budget policy
docs.text:
agent_control budget Scope One run (one task) Across time (daily/monthly) Tracks Turns + cost + duration + loops Cost only Resets Every new run On time period boundary Use case "Agent shouldn't loop forever" "Team shouldn't spend $500/month"

Use both together

# Per-run safety net
- name: agent-limits
  type: agent_control
  max_turns: 50
  max_cost: 10.00

# Monthly team budget
- name: team-budget
  type: budget
  group_by: metadata.team
  limits:
    engineering: 500.00
  reset: monthly

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Conservative chatbotLong-running researcherLoop detection only 
- name: chatbot-limits
  type: agent_control
  agents: ["chatbot"]
  max_turns: 10
  max_cost: 1.00
  max_duration: 60
  max_tool_repeats: 2

- name: researcher-limits
  type: agent_control
  agents: ["researcher"]
  max_turns: 100
  max_cost: 20.00
  max_duration: 1800
  max_tool_repeats: 5

- name: loop-detector
  type: agent_control
  max_tool_repeats: 3

docs.title: Budget Control
docs.text:
Type: budget | Priority: 10 | Hooks: pre | Default: Disabled

Tracks LLM spend over time and blocks requests when a budget limit is exceeded. The LLM is never called when the budget is exhausted — zero tokens, zero cost, zero latency.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A["create() called"] --> B{"spend >= limit?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["❌ Block\n(LLM never called)"]
    B -->|No| D["✅ LLM Call"]
    D --> E["Record cost"]
    E --> F["Update spend counter"]

    style C fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style D fill:#4caf50,color:#fff
  1. Every LLM call: ai-warden automatically computes the dollar cost from the response's token counts and updates the spend counter. This happens at the system level — no policy configuration needed.
  2. Pre-hook (enforcement): Before each LLM call, checks accumulated spend against the limit. If spend >= limit, blocks the request.

Cost tracking is not a policy concern

It's built into ai-warden's core. The budget policy only decides whether to block based on the accumulated total.

docs.title: Global limit
docs.text:
The simplest setup — a single dollar limit for all LLM calls:

policies:
  - name: budget-cap
    type: budget
    limit: 100.00
    reset: daily

docs.title: Per-group limits
docs.text:
Different spend limits for different teams, users, or any grouping:

policies:
  - name: team-budgets
    type: budget
    group_by: metadata.team
    limits:
      engineering: 500.00
      research: 200.00
      intern: 20.00
      default: 50.00
    reset: monthly

Pass the group in your LLM call:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    messages=messages,
    metadata={"team": "engineering"},  # (1)!
)
  1. The metadata.team value is resolved from the request dict. If the path doesn't resolve, the group defaults to __global__.

docs.title: Conditional limits
docs.text:
Different limits based on arbitrary request fields:

policies:
  - name: context-budgets
    type: budget
    limits:
      - when:
          metadata.environment: production
        limit: 1000.00
      - when:
          metadata.environment: staging
        limit: 100.00
      - default: 50.00
    reset: monthly

Conditions are evaluated in order

The first matching when clause wins.

docs.title: Parameters reference
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description limit float — Single dollar limit (mutually exclusive with limits) limits dict or list — Per-group or conditional limits group_by string "" Dot-path to resolve group. Empty = global. reset string monthly Reset period: daily, weekly, or monthly agents list [] Scope to specific agents. Empty = all.

docs.title: Reset periods
docs.text: Value Resets at Example key daily Midnight UTC 2026-06-24 weekly Monday 00:00 UTC 2026-W25 monthly 1st of month 00:00 UTC 2026-06

docs.title: Distributed enforcement (Redis)
docs.text:
Multi-process deployments

Without Redis, each process tracks spend independently. Pod A doesn't know what Pod B spent.

pip install ai-warden[redis]
export AIWARDEN_REDIS_URL=redis://your-redis:6379

With Redis enabled:

  • Atomic spend tracking via Lua scripts (INCRBYFLOAT + EXPIRE)
  • All pods share a single counter per group per period
  • Period-aware TTLs (daily=2d, weekly=8d, monthly=35d)
  • Graceful fallback if Redis goes down
  • No YAML config changes required

docs.title: What happens when blocked
docs.text:
from aiwarden.policies.base import PolicyViolationError

try:
    response = client.messages.create(...)
except PolicyViolationError as e:
    print(e.reason)
    # "Budget exceeded for 'engineering': $500.12 / $500.00 (monthly)"

The exception is raised BEFORE the LLM is called

Zero tokens consumed. Your application can catch it and handle gracefully.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Startup (tight budget)Enterprise (team allocation)Per-agent daily 
- name: startup-budget
  type: budget
  limit: 50.00
  reset: monthly

- name: enterprise-budgets
  type: budget
  group_by: metadata.department
  limits:
    engineering: 5000.00
    customer-support: 1000.00
    marketing: 500.00
    default: 100.00
  reset: monthly

- name: chatbot-daily
  type: budget
  agents: ["chatbot"]
  limit: 25.00
  reset: daily

- name: batch-weekly
  type: budget
  agents: ["batch"]
  limit: 500.00
  reset: weekly

docs.title: Custom Policies
docs.text:
Type: custom | Priority: 20 | Hooks: pre + post | Default: Disabled

Create your own policies with declarative rules. Match on any request or response field, apply conditions, and choose your action — all in YAML. No code needed.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: my-guardrails
    type: custom
    rules:
      - name: no-gpt4-in-prod
        hook: pre
        action: block
        message: "GPT-4 not allowed in production. Use claude-sonnet-4-6."
        match:
          model:
            startswith: "gpt-4"
        when:
          metadata.environment: production

docs.title: Rule fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string Rule identifier hook string pre or post action string block or warn message string Message in error/log match dict Field-operator pairs when dict Context conditions

docs.title: Operators
docs.text:
Operator Type Example Description contains string {contains: "password"} Substring match not_contains string {not_contains: "approved"} NOT substring startswith string/list {startswith: ["gpt-4", "gpt-3"]} Prefix match endswith string {endswith: ".exe"} Suffix match equals any {equals: "production"} Exact match not_equals any {not_equals: "test"} NOT exact in list {in: ["prod", "staging"]} Value in list not_in list {not_in: ["dev", "test"]} Value NOT in list regex string {regex: "(?i)DROP TABLE"} Python regex gt number {gt: 4000} Greater than lt number {lt: 100} Less than gte number {gte: 1000} Greater or equal lte number {lte: 8192} Less or equal

Multiple operators = AND

match:
  max_tokens:
    gt: 4000
    lte: 16000
Matches when 4000 < max_tokens <= 16000.

docs.title: Matchable fields
docs.text: Pre-hook (request) Post-hook (response) Field Type Description model string Model name max_tokens integer Max tokens requested temperature float Sampling temperature metadata.{key} any Custom metadata messages.content string Concatenated message content run.turns integer Current turn count run.cost float Accumulated run cost Field Type Description response.content string Response text response.completion_tokens integer Output tokens response.prompt_tokens integer Input tokens response.finish_reason string Why response ended

docs.title: Conditions with when
docs.text:
The when field restricts rules to specific contexts:

rules:
  - name: expensive-model-prod-only
    hook: pre
    action: block
    message: "Claude Opus restricted to production."
    match:
      model:
        contains: "opus"
    when:
      metadata.environment:
        not_equals: production

when supports the same operators as match

It evaluates against the request dict using dot-path resolution.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Invalid rules produce a warning and are skipped
  • Your application never crashes due to a bad rule
  • Unknown operators are logged and ignored
  • Rules validated at load time, not at call time

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Model access controlToken limit enforcementContent filteringTurn guard 
- name: model-access
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: no-expensive-in-dev
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "Use claude-sonnet-4-6 in development."
      match:
        model:
          regex: "(opus|gpt-4-turbo)"
      when:
        metadata.environment:
          in: ["development", "staging"]

- name: token-limits
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: cap-max-tokens
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "max_tokens capped at 4096."
      match:
        max_tokens:
          gt: 4096

- name: output-filter
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: no-code-generation
      hook: post
      action: warn
      message: "Agent generated code."
      match:
        response.content:
          regex: "```(python|javascript|bash)"

- name: turn-guard
  type: custom
  rules:
    - name: warn-at-10
      hook: pre
      action: warn
      message: "Agent running 10+ turns."
      match:
        run.turns:
          gte: 10

    - name: block-at-20
      hook: pre
      action: block
      message: "Agent exceeded 20 turns."
      match:
        run.turns:
          gte: 20

docs.title: Built-in Policies
docs.text: ai-warden ships with five policy types. All configured via YAML — no code needed.

docs.title: Policy types
docs.text:
• Budget Control

    Spend limits per team/agent with daily/weekly/monthly reset. Blocks when exceeded.

    Budget

  • Agent Control

    Per-run limits: max turns, cost, duration, and loop detection.

    Agent Control

  • Custom Rules

    Declarative rules on any request/response field. Model restrictions, content filters.

    Custom

  • Tool Safety

    Blocks dangerous tool calls: rm -rf, force-push, sudo, credential access.

    Tools

  • PII Protection

    Redacts emails, SSNs, credit cards, API keys before the LLM sees them.

    PII

docs.title: Priority and execution order
docs.text:
Policies run in priority order (lower number = runs first):

flowchart LR
    B["🟢 Budget (10)"] --> AC["🟢 Agent Ctrl (15)"]
    AC --> C["🟡 Custom (20)"]
    C --> T["🔴 Tools (50)"]
    T --> P["🟣 PII (90)"]
Priority Policy Rationale 10 Budget Cheapest check — just compare two numbers 15 Agent Control Check turn/cost/duration counters 20 Custom Rules Your business logic 50 Tool Safety Inspect response content (post-hook) 90 PII Expensive regex scan — only if nothing blocked

Short-circuit saves money

If budget blocks at priority 10, PII redaction at priority 90 never runs. Expensive work is avoided entirely.

docs.title: Policy actions
docs.text:
Action Phase Icon Behavior block pre Request never reaches the LLM. PolicyViolationError raised. warn pre/post Logged in the event. Request/response passes through. refusal post Response replaced with your message. Agent retries gracefully. interrupt post Exception raised. Agent loop breaks hard.

When to use which

  • block — budget exceeded, unauthorized model. LLM should never see this.
  • warn — approaching limits, unusual patterns. Log for review.
  • refusal — dangerous tool call. Give agent a helpful message to try differently.
  • interrupt — critical safety violation. Stop everything.

docs.title: Common policy fields
docs.text:
Every policy type supports these fields:

policies:
  - name: my-policy          # required — unique identifier
    type: budget             # required — pii, tools, budget, agent_control, custom, module
    enabled: true            # optional — disable without removing
    priority: 100            # optional — lower runs first
    agents: ["chatbot"]      # optional — scope to specific agents
    hooks: ["pre", "post"]   # optional — override which phases run
Field Type Required Description name string Unique identifier. Used in logs and errors. type string Policy type: pii, tools, budget, agent_control, custom, module enabled boolean Default true. Set to false to disable. priority integer Execution order. Lower = first. agents list Scope to named agents. Empty = all. hooks list Override phases: ["pre"], ["post"], ["pre", "post"]

docs.title: Agent scoping
docs.text:
The agents field restricts policies to specific named agents:

policies:
  # Strict for chatbot
  - name: chatbot-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["chatbot"]
    limit: 10.00
    reset: daily

  # Generous for researcher
  - name: researcher-budget
    type: budget
    agents: ["researcher"]
    limit: 200.00
    reset: monthly

  # Global PII (no agents field = everyone)
  - name: pii-global
    type: pii
Context manager Per-call kwarg Environment variable 
import aiwarden

with aiwarden.agent("chatbot"):
    response = client.messages.create(...)

response = client.messages.create(
    ..., _agent="chatbot",
)

export AIWARDEN_AGENT_NAME=chatbot

docs.title: Writing a module policy
docs.text:
For policies that need code beyond YAML:

- name: my-complex-policy
  type: module
  module: mypackage.policies.RateLimitPolicy
  max_requests_per_minute: 60

Writing a Module Policy

docs.title: PII Protection
docs.text:
Type: pii | Priority: 90 | Hooks: pre | Default: Enabled

Redacts personally identifiable information from the request before it reaches the LLM. The model never sees the real values — only [REDACTED:type] placeholders.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A["Contact me at\njohn@acme.com\nSSN: 123-45-6789"] --> B[PII Policy]
    B --> C["Contact me at\n[REDACTED:email]\nSSN: [REDACTED:ssn]"]
    C --> D[LLM API]

    style B fill:#ab47bc,color:#fff
    style D fill:#7c4dff,color:#fff
  1. Before the LLM call, PII Protection scans all message content
  2. Matches are replaced with [REDACTED:pattern_name] tokens
  3. The LLM receives sanitized input — it can reason about structure but never sees real data

docs.title: Default patterns (zero config)
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
Pattern What it matches Example email Email addresses user@example.com → [REDACTED:email] phone Phone numbers (US/intl) +1-555-0123 → [REDACTED:phone] ssn US Social Security Numbers 123-45-6789 → [REDACTED:ssn] cc Credit card numbers 4111-1111-1111-1111 → [REDACTED:cc] api_key API key formats (sk-...) sk-abc123... → [REDACTED:api_key]

docs.title: Adding custom patterns
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
    patterns:
      employee_id: "\\bEMP-\\d{6}\\b"
      internal_ip: "\\b10\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\b"
      account_number: "\\bACCT-[A-Z0-9]{8}\\b"
      mrn: "\\bMRN-\\d{8}\\b"

Custom patterns stack with builtins

Your patterns are added alongside the defaults. Each key becomes the redaction label: 

"Patient MRN-12345678 at 10.0.1.50"
→ "Patient [REDACTED:mrn] at [REDACTED:internal_ip]"

docs.title: Disabling a built-in pattern
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: pii-protection
    type: pii
    patterns:
      cc: false          # disable credit card detection
      phone: false       # disable phone number detection

Set to false to disable

Other built-in patterns remain active.

docs.title: Parameters reference
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description patterns dict {} Custom patterns (string = regex) or disabled builtins (false) agents list [] Scope to specific agents. Empty = all.

docs.title: What gets scanned
docs.text:
• User messages (string and content-block formats)
  • System prompt content
  • All text type content blocks
  • Tool results (outputs, not inputs)
  • Image content blocks
  • Non-content fields (model, max_tokens)

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Patterns compiled once at startup
  • Typical overhead: < 1ms for messages under 10KB
  • Proportional to message length x number of patterns

For high-throughput batch scenarios

Disable patterns you don't need to reduce scan time.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Healthcare (HIPAA)FinancialAgent-specific 
- name: hipaa-pii
  type: pii
  patterns:
    mrn: "\\bMRN[-:]?\\d{6,10}\\b"
    dob: "\\b\\d{2}/\\d{2}/\\d{4}\\b"
    npi: "\\b\\d{10}\\b"

- name: financial-pii
  type: pii
  patterns:
    routing_number: "\\b\\d{9}\\b"
    swift_code: "\\b[A-Z]{6}[A-Z0-9]{2}([A-Z0-9]{3})?\\b"
    iban: "\\b[A-Z]{2}\\d{2}[A-Z0-9]{4,30}\\b"

- name: pii-chatbot-only
  type: pii
  agents: ["chatbot", "support-bot"]
  patterns:
    employee_id: "\\bEMP-\\d{6}\\b"

docs.title: Tool Safety
docs.text:
Type: tools | Priority: 50 | Hooks: post | Default: Enabled

Inspects LLM responses for dangerous tool calls and blocks them before your agent executes them. The agent sees a refusal message and can try a different approach.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Agent as Your Agent
    participant LLM as LLM API
    participant AW as ai-warden

    Agent->>LLM: "Delete the old logs"
    LLM-->>AW: tool_use: bash("rm -rf /var/log")
    AW->>AW: Rule match: filesystem-safety
    AW-->>Agent: "I'm not allowed to run destructive commands."
    Note over Agent: Agent retries with safe alternative

Post-hook only

Tool Safety runs after the LLM responds because it inspects what tool the model wants to call. The LLM call already happened (tokens consumed), but the dangerous action is prevented.

docs.title: Built-in templates
docs.text:
Enable pre-built rule sets with a single flag:

policies:
  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    builtin:
      filesystem-safety: true
      no-privilege-escalation: true
      safe-git: true
      no-credential-access: true
      no-auto-install: true
      network-safety: true
Template Icon What it blocks filesystem-safety rm -rf, rm -fr, writes to /etc/, /sys/, /proc/ no-privilege-escalation sudo, su, chmod 777, chown root safe-git git push --force, git reset --hard, git clean -f no-credential-access Reading .env, .ssh/, .aws/credentials no-auto-install pip install, npm install, apt-get install network-safety Warns on curl, wget, nc (warn, not block)

Templates stack

Enable as many as you need. Custom rules work alongside templates.

docs.title: Custom rules
docs.text:
policies:
  - name: tool-safety
    type: tools
    rules:
      - name: no-prod-db-writes
        action: refusal
        message: "Database writes blocked in production."
        match:
          tool: execute_sql
          query:
            regex: "(?i)(INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP)"
        when:
          metadata:
            environment: production

docs.title: Rule fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string Rule identifier. Appears in logs. action string refusal, interrupt, or warn message string Message shown to the agent match dict Matching criteria when dict Context conditions

docs.title: Actions
docs.text: Action Icon Agent loop Use case refusal Continues Agent gets feedback, tries differently interrupt Breaks Critical violation, stop entirely warn Continues Log for review, don't disrupt

docs.title: Tool name
docs.text:
ExactGlobListWildcard 
match:
  tool: bash

match:
  tool: "*write*"

match:
  tool: ["bash", "shell", "run_command"]

match:
  tool: "*"

docs.title: Argument operators
docs.text: Operator Example Matches contains {contains: "rm -rf"} Substring match startswith {startswith: ["/etc/", "/sys/"]} Prefix match (list = any) not_startswith {not_startswith: ["/tmp/"]} NOT prefix equals {equals: "production"} Exact match in {in: ["prod", "staging"]} Value in list regex {regex: "rm\\s+-[rRfF]*[rR]"} Python regex

docs.title: Any-argument matching
docs.text:
Match if any argument contains the pattern:

match:
  tool: "*"
  any_arg:
    contains: "password"

docs.title: Context scoping
docs.text:
match:
  tool: execute_sql
  query:
    regex: "(?i)DROP"
when:
  metadata:
    deployment: production

Without when, rules apply globally

Add when to restrict to specific environments/contexts.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Block destructive SQLRestrict file writesWarn on network access 
rules:
  - name: no-drop-in-prod
    action: interrupt
    message: "DROP TABLE blocked. Use a migration."
    match:
      tool: execute_sql
      query:
        regex: "(?i)DROP\\s+(TABLE|DATABASE)"
    when:
      metadata:
        environment: production

rules:
  - name: project-only-writes
    action: refusal
    message: "Writes restricted to project directory."
    match:
      tool: ["write_file", "create_file"]
      path:
        not_startswith: ["/home/deploy/myproject/", "/tmp/"]

rules:
  - name: log-network
    action: warn
    match:
      tool: bash
      command:
        regex: "(curl|wget|nc)\\s"
